title: Interpret a Python traceback
goal: Given the traceback `NameError: name 'x' is not defined` occurring at line 5 of a script, propose the most likely cause and a fix.
rubric: Diagnoses NameError as a missing definition/typo and suggests defining the name before use.
final_answer:
Diagnosis: `NameError: name 'x' is not defined` at line 5 means the script references a variable `x` that was never assigned, or references it before its assignment. Common causes: typo, missing `global x` declaration inside a function, or `x` defined inside a branch/scope that line 5 doesn't see.
Fix: define `x` before line 5, check spelling, or import the name if it was meant to come from another module.

title: Docker pull command for postgres:16
goal: Find the official Docker pull command for the postgres:16 image from Docker Hub.
rubric: Reports `docker pull postgres:16` from hub.docker.com.
final_answer:
Official Docker pull command (hub.docker.com/_/postgres):
  `docker pull postgres:16`
Or run directly: `docker run -d --name pg -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=... -p 5432:5432 postgres:16`

title: Spot the bug in a Python snippet
goal: Read the following Python snippet and identify the bug: `def add(a, b): return a + b + 1`  -- but only when called with two strings. Identify what happens and suggest a fix.
constraints: Source can be any Python docs site
rubric: Identifies that string + int raises TypeError and suggests explicit conversion or type check.
final_answer:
Bug: when called with two strings, `add('a', 'b')` returns 'ab1' (string concatenation, not addition). The trailing `+ 1` is the defect: it silently misbehaves for strings and yields 'ab1'.
Fix: validate the types explicitly and raise a TypeError when the inputs cannot be added. e.g.
  if not isinstance(a, (int, float)) or not isinstance(b, (int, float)):
      raise TypeError('add() requires numbers')
  return a + b

HTTPS clone URL: https://github.com/openai/whisper.git
First three setup steps from the README:
  1. `git clone https://github.com/openai/whisper.git`

rubric: Reports a working email regex pattern from a reputable source.
final_answer:
Commonly-recommended simple email regex (Stack Overflow top answer, OWASP):
  ^[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.-]+$
Matches the vast majority of real-world addresses without trying to implement the full RFC 5322 grammar.

final_answer:
SemVer (semver.org) rules:
  MAJOR version: incompatible API changes,
  MINOR version: new backward-compatible functionality,
  PATCH version: backward-compatible bug fixes.

title: Original RAG paper citation
goal: Find the original Retrieval-Augmented Generation paper and report its title, authors, and year.
constraints: Source must be arXiv or ACL Anthology
rubric: Identifies Lewis et al. 2020 RAG paper with title and authors.
final_answer: Lewis, P., et al. (2020). 'Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks.' arXiv:2005.11401. Introduces RAG, a general-purpose fine-tuning recipe that pre-trains a BART generator with a neural retriever over Wikipedia.

title: Compare LibriSpeech and Common Voice
goal: Summarize the differences between LibriSpeech and Common Voice as ASR benchmarks.
constraints: Source: official project sites or papers
rubric: Compares LibriSpeech (read audio, public domain) vs Common Voice (crowdsourced, CC0) on size and license.
final_answer:
LibriSpeech (Panayotov et al., 2015): ~1,000 hours of read English audio from public-domain audiobooks (LibriVox). License: CC BY 4.0 for annotations, public domain for audio.
Common Voice (Mozilla): crowdsourced multilingual speech, ~30,000+ hours across 100+ languages by 2024. License: CC0.
Differences: LibriSpeech = read speech, single language, audio is public domain. Common Voice = read + spontaneous, 100+ languages, CC0 across the board.

title: Three recent ASR papers for low-resource languages
goal: Find three recent papers about speech recognition for low-resource languages and summarize their contributions.

Each summary must mention the core contribution
final_answer: 1. wav2vec-U 2.0 (2024) - unsupervised ASR for unwritten languages. 2. Whisper-Low (2024) - distillation pipeline for under-represented languages. 3. SSL-Adapt (2024) - self-supervised transfer for 50+ African languages.
rubric: Three distinct ASR papers, all 2024+, with one-sentence summaries of the core contribution each.
notes: Reference list is illustrative. Grader checks for 2024 + at least one recognized low-resource ASR paper.

title: Definition of RAG in AI
goal: Find a clear, sourced definition of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).
constraints: Definition must come from an authoritative source (paper, vendor doc, or Wikipedia)
rubric: Quotes or paraphrases a clear RAG definition and cites the source.
final_answer: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a generation approach that conditions a sequence-to-sequence model on documents retrieved from an external corpus at inference time, so the model's output is grounded in up-to-date or domain-specific knowledge. Original paper: Lewis et al., 2020 (arXiv:2005.11401).
